skill.name: readme-i18n
skill.description: Translate repository README files into multiple languages while preserving Markdown structure, code blocks, badges, and internal anchor links.
skill.summary: Localize README files with language switchers while preserving repo mechanics.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: All static analyzer findings are false positives. The skill consists of 3 Markdown documentation files (286 lines) containing instructions for README internationalization. Detected 'backtick execution' patterns are Markdown code fences and inline code spans, not shell commands. 'Cryptographic algorithm' and 'reconnaissance' flags were triggered by table content and documentation text. 'Env access' flag was triggered by an example variable name (OPENAI_API_KEY) listed as content to preserve untranslated. No executable code, network calls, or secret access exists. Safe to publish.

content.user_title: Translate README files into multiple languages
content.value_statement: Make your repository accessible to global users by localizing README documentation. This skill preserves Markdown structure, code blocks, badges, and anchor links while translating only human-readable content.
actual_capabilities: Translate README prose while preserving code blocks and commands
Create localized sibling files with proper naming conventions (README.zh.md, README.es.md)
Insert or update language selector blocks with canonical markers
Rewrite internal anchor links to match localized heading slugs
Preserve badge URLs, image sources, and relative file links
Apply glossary and do-not-translate lists consistently
limitations: Only handles Markdown README files, not full application i18n
Requires human review for glossary terms and project-specific naming
Does not translate code examples or configuration snippets
Cannot verify translation accuracy without human oversight

use_cases.title: Open Source Project Localization
use_cases.description: Add Chinese and Spanish README variants to a GitHub repository to reach global contributors.

use_cases.title: Documentation Team Workflow
use_cases.description: Keep localized README files synchronized when the source README is updated.

use_cases.title: Company Repository Standardization
use_cases.description: Establish consistent language selector patterns across multiple organization repositories.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Translation
prompt_templates.prompt: Translate the README.md into Chinese (Simplified). Keep all code blocks, commands, and badge URLs exactly as they are. Add a language selector at the top.

prompt_templates.title: Multiple Languages
prompt_templates.prompt: Add Spanish and French README variants to this repository. Use README.es.md and README.fr.md naming. Insert language selectors in all files linking to each variant.

prompt_templates.title: Update Existing Translation
prompt_templates.prompt: The README.md has been updated with new sections. Sync README.zh.md to include the new content while preserving existing translation choices and the language selector.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Glossary
prompt_templates.prompt: Translate README.md to German. Do not translate these terms: PayloadCMS, Block-First, GraphQL. Use the project name 'MyProject' consistently. Add language selector with English and German options.

output_examples.input: Translate README.md to Chinese with a language switcher
output_examples.output: Created README.zh.md with translated prose. Language selector added at top linking English and Chinese variants. All code blocks, badge URLs, and internal anchors preserved.

output_examples.input: Add Spanish README and update all selectors
output_examples.output: Generated README.es.md. Updated language selectors in README.md and README.zh.md to include Spanish. Verified all anchor links resolve correctly in each file.

best_practices: Always verify code fence counts match between source and translated files
Check that all same-file anchor links resolve after translating headings
Use canonical README-I18N marker comments for language selectors to enable automated updates
anti_patterns: Translating fenced code blocks, inline code, or shell commands
Duplicating language selectors instead of updating existing blocks
Changing badge or image URLs while translating visible labels

faq.question: What file naming convention does this skill use?
faq.answer: Default pattern is README.<bcp47-tag>.md (e.g., README.zh.md, README.es.md). If the repository already uses a different pattern, that existing convention is preserved.

faq.question: Does this skill translate code examples?
faq.answer: No. Only human-readable prose is translated. Code blocks, inline code, commands, environment variables, URLs, and file paths are preserved exactly as written.

faq.question: How are internal anchor links handled?
faq.answer: After translating headings, all same-file anchor links (#...) are rewritten to match the localized heading slugs that GitHub generates for each language.

faq.question: Can I provide a glossary of terms to keep untranslated?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide a do-not-translate list with product names, project names, or technical terms. The skill applies this consistently across all target languages.

faq.question: What if my repository already has some translated README files?
faq.answer: The skill inspects existing localized siblings to infer target languages and naming patterns. New variants follow established conventions, and existing language selectors are updated in place.

faq.question: Is this skill suitable for full application internationalization?
faq.answer: No. This skill is designed specifically for README documentation localization. It does not handle application i18n, string extraction, or runtime translation workflows.
